exemplar.title: Will the effort heuristic influence the quality perception of art?

detailedExemplarData.title: Will the effort heuristic influence the quality perception of art?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement of the investigation aim and its relevance to cognitive bias in art valuation
Accurate description of System 1 vs System 2 and the effort heuristic with explicit link to the study
Wellâoperationalized independent and dependent variables and precise null and alternative hypotheses
weaknesses: Real-world implications of the investigation are addressed but somewhat briefly
Minor citation inaccuracy (misspelling âKanhemanâ) undermines scholarly rigor
Could reinforce the link between the effort heuristic and study aim more explicitly in the theory section
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction consistently meets topâband descriptors in aim relevance and theory linkage, and fully operationalizes the variables with precise hypotheses. A small citation error and a slightly cursory treatment of broader implications prevent a full 6/6 rating, resulting in a score of 5/6.

strengths: Sampling technique and participant characteristics are clearly described and justified
Independent-measures design is explained with reasons to minimize demand characteristics
Controlled variables and materials are not only listed but their rationale for maintaining causality is given
weaknesses: Use of ambiguous symbols (e.g., Ïâââ) in procedure description reduces clarity
Participant variation in perceiving effort labels is not fully acknowledged in operationalization
Details from the pre-trial and planned coding of qualitative responses are not fully elaborated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section delivers an explained research design, sampling rationale, and control procedures, firmly placing it in the upper band (3â4). Some clarity issues and missing methodological details keep it from the top of the band, earning 3/4.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive statistics and an independent-samples t-test directly addressing the hypothesis
Graphical presentation (bar charts with error bars) effectively compares groups
Interpretation of p-values is accurate and clearly linked to the effort heuristic theory
weaknesses: Minor presentation errors: commaâstyle decimals, truncated axis labels, missing effect sizes
Inconsistent chart captions and unclear pooled-variance calculations
No specification of one-tailed vs. two-tailed t-test to match directional hypotheses
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analyses and graphical methods are appropriate and largely accurate (band 3â4), and findings are interpreted in direct relation to the hypothesis (band 5â6). The presence of multiple small presentation errors prevents a topâband score, resulting in 4/6.

strengths: Discussion of findings refers back to Kruger et al. and System 1/2 theory
Strengths and limitations are identified and linked to the investigationâs design
Some constructive suggestions for future research are offered
weaknesses: Modifications are stated vaguely and not explicitly tied to the limitations discussed
The depth of theoretical implication and alternative explanations is limited
No concrete, justified plan (e.g., stratified sampling) to address identified weaknesses
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation includes a theory-referenced discussion and relevant strengths/limitations (band 3â4) but fails to provide fully justified, limitation-linked modifications (band 1â2). This mixed performance yields a mid-band score of 3/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Three bar charts with error bars directly compare painting ratings across groups, clearly addressing the hypothesis.  Axes are labelled, yet titles are duplicated and one chart has truncated y-axis labels, so minor errors are present.

The student states that p < 0.05 for all measures, explicitly rejects the null and affirms the research hypothesis, linking the statistical outcome to effort heuristic theory.  Interpretation is accurate and fully connected to the data, though brief, justifying a high-band score.

Both IV (time label: 4 h vs 26 h) and DV (perceived quality measured by Likert ratings and assigned monetary value) are not only identified but operationalised.  Separate null and alternative hypotheses are written in precise, testable form, fulfilling all top-band requirements.

Convenience sampling is defined, participant characteristics (age, school level, exclusion of psychology students) are presented, and pros/cons for generalisability are discussed.  That constitutes an explanation of choice, placing the work in the upper band for this strand.

Heuristics, System 1 vs System 2 and specifically the effort heuristic are described accurately, with citations to Kahneman & Tversky and Kruger et al.  The text then explicitly states that the experiment replicates Kruger to test whether perceived effort raises quality judgements, so the link between theory and investigation is clearly explained.

The discussion section relates findings back to Kruger et al. and to System 1/2 theory, but the treatment is short and largely confirmatory without deeper theoretical implications or alternative explanations.  This meets the âdescribed with referenceâ level.

The student clearly states the aim: to investigate whether perceived effort (time stated) influences enjoyment/quality ratings of paintings.  The introduction links this aim to everyday decision-making and to replicated research, explaining why the question is relevant for understanding cognitive bias in art valuation.  The explanation is coherent but somewhat brief on real-world implications, so the work fits solidly in the explained category without exceptional depth.

Strengths and limitations concerning sampling diversity, gender imbalance, independent measures, and monetary valuation are identified and described with relevance to the investigation, but explanations lack nuance (e.g., no detail on effect sizes), fitting the mid-band.

Key controlled variables (presentation wording, viewing time, environment) are listed with brief rationale, and materials are enumerated.  The student explains why each control is necessary to maintain causality, meeting the âexplainedâ criterion.

Only vague future-research comments are offered (e.g., âarea for future researchâ, âlack of justification for answersâ).  Modifications are not explicitly linked to the stated limitations nor fully justified, so the strand falls into the lowest non-zero band.

The independent-measures design is spelled out with justification (avoiding demand characteristics) and acknowledgement of individual-difference weaknesses, satisfying the rubricâs expectation of explanation rather than mere description.

Descriptive statistics (means, SDs) and an independent-samples t-test are appropriate for the data.  Worked formulas and p-values are provided, but there are minor presentation issues (comma/decimal confusion, unclear pooled-variance calculation).  These small errors prevent full accuracy yet remain within the âappropriate but with errorsâ band.

annotations.comment: The title is concise and clearly conveys the core topic and aim of investigating the effort heuristic in art perception, providing immediate context for the reader.
In your theory section, explicitly link the effort heuristic to your studyâs aim (perceived effort labels influencing enjoyment) to reinforce relevance.
The description of Kruger et al.âs Cornell sample conflicts with your own; clarify whether this refers to the original study or your replication.
The claim that your independentâmeasures design enhances both generalizability and ecological validity conflates two constructs; separate and justify each term.
The table of contents omits page numbers and uses uneven formatting; include accurate section pagination and consistent styling to improve navigation.
Use uniform heading styles and ensure every entry in the contents is paired with its corresponding page number for clarity.
The misspelling âKanhemanâ undermines citation accuracy; correct this to âKahnemanâ to maintain scholarly rigor.
Remove ambiguous symbols (e.g., \(\omega_{\rm max}\)) from your controls list to maintain clarity in describing materials and procedures.
Labeling the IV as the âonly variable that will changeâ overlooks potential interpretive differences; acknowledge participant variation in your operationalisation.
Detail the specific issues uncovered in your pre-trial and how they informed changes to your experimental protocol.
Explain how qualitative responses from your open-ended questions will be coded or analysed to enhance methodological transparency.
Justify the choice of personal devices and Google Forms, explaining how they standardise data collection and reduce experimenter influence.
Include the actual gender breakdown of participants to illustrate and assess the impact of the noted imbalance on your findings.
The descriptive-statistics section describes generic concepts rather than summarising your studyâs specific mean and SD; contextualise with your actual values.
Present summary sentences (e.g., âGroup 1 enjoyment for Painting A: M=4.00, SD=2.10â) immediately before tables for clearer data interpretation.
Table 1 uses commaâstyle decimals (e.g., â7,17â); switch to period notation for standard statistical formatting.
Charts lack full axis labels and units in Figures 1 and 2; label each axis completely and include measurement scales.
Enhance chart captions by briefly stating the key outcome (e.g., which painting was rated higher) to guide reader interpretation.
Figure 2 on page 9 is missing a caption and has truncated labels; add a descriptive title and full axis annotation.
Specify whether a one-tailed or two-tailed t-test was used, given your directional hypothesis, to clarify inferential statistics choices.
Report effect sizes (e.g., Cohenâs d) alongside t-values to quantify the magnitude of differences and strengthen findings interpretation.
Evaluation omits explicit modifications tied to limitations; recommend detailing specific changes (e.g., stratified sampling) to address each weakness.
Propose including forced justification prompts for open-ended questions to mitigate the limitation of lacking answer rationale.
The bibliography mixes citation styles and inconsistent formatting; adopt a single, recognized referencing convention (e.g., APA) throughout.
